perturbation.original_text: Each Party receiving Confidential Information under this Agreement shall maintain the information in confidence in accordance with the terms of this Agreement notwithstanding any termination of this Agreement for a period of three years from the date the Confidential Information is disclosed to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party receiving Confidential Information under this Agreement shall maintain the information in confidence in accordance with the terms of this Agreement notwithstanding any termination of this Agreement for a period of one year from the date the Confidential Information is disclosed to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Michigan law, potentially conflicting with the reasonable measures required to maintain trade secret status.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA) requires trade secret holders to take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of their information. Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may not be considered a reasonable measure, especially for sensitive trade secrets, potentially jeopardizing their legal protection under the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The change reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that this reduction may not be considered a 'reasonable measure' under the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA) to protect trade secrets, potentially jeopardizing their legal protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1, MCL 445.1903, discusses injunctions for misappropriation and the elimination of commercial advantage. Reducing the confidentiality period could be seen as failing to take reasonable measures, potentially conflicting with the requirements of the MUTSA to protect trade secrets, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information delivered by either Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party. No copies shall be made without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. All Confidential Information including the copies made thereof is the sole property of the Disclosing Party and shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed upon written request by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information delivered by either Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Copies can be made freely. All Confidential Information including the copies made thereof is the sole property of the Disclosing Party and need not be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the free reproduction and retention of confidential information contradicts the principle of trade secret protection under Michigan law, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing copies to be made freely and removing the obligation to return or destroy the information, the receiving party is not taking reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of the information. This undermines the basis for trade secret protection under MUTSA, as it fails to maintain the confidentiality required for trade secret status.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1902 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1902 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1902 Definitions. Sec. 2. As used in this act: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach, or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or any other means. (b) "Misappropriation" means either of the following: (i) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. (ii) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who did 1 or more of the following: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means an individual, corporation, partnership, association, governmental entity, or any other legal entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that is both of the following: (i) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. (ii) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History: 1998, Act 44
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the requirement to maintain secrecy of trade secrets, a core element for protection under Michigan law. By allowing free reproduction and removing the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the modified contract fails to meet the "reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy" standard required by MCL 445.1902(d)(ii). The original text mandated control over copies and return or destruction of confidential information, whereas the changed text explicitly permits uncontrolled copying and retention, thereby undermining any claim to trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement pertains only to the Confidential Information that is disclosed between the Effective Date (last signature date) and one year from that date. Each Party receiving Confidential Information under this Agreement shall maintain the information in confidence in accordance with the terms of this Agreement notwithstanding any termination of this Agreement for a period of three years from the date the Confidential Information is disclosed to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement pertains only to the Confidential Information that is disclosed between the Effective Date (last signature date) and one year from that date. Each Party receiving Confidential Information under this Agreement shall maintain the information in confidence in accordance with the terms of this Agreement notwithstanding any termination of this Agreement for a period of six months from the date the Confidential Information is disclosed to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the period for maintaining confidentiality to six months from the date of disclosure may not provide adequate protection for trade secrets, potentially violating the requirements for reasonable measures to maintain secrecy under Michigan law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires that reasonable steps be taken to maintain the secrecy of a trade secret. Reducing the confidentiality period to six months may be considered unreasonable for certain types of information, therefore jeopardizing trade secret protection under the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from three years to six months. The Law Explanation says that a reduced confidentiality period 'may be considered unreasonable for certain types of information, therefore jeopardizing trade secret protection under the law.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act, discusses misappropriation and injunctions. It indicates that injunctions can be continued 'for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation.' The Perturbation Explanation states this change may not provide adequate protection for trade secrets, potentially violating the requirements for reasonable measures to maintain secrecy. Thus, reducing the confidentiality period could contradict the requirement to take reasonable steps to protect trade secrets, making the terms unenforceable.
